activities.name: The Soft Snack Attack
activities.description: Learn to bring up a complaint without blame or criticism.
activities.detailedDescription: This level teaches you to replace "Blame" with "Soft Start-ups" to keep your heart rate calm and your partner's ears open.
steps: 1. The "I" Ingredient. Instead of saying "You always pick pizza," say "I feel really excited when we try something fresh and crunchy." Goal: To focus on your own internal feelings so your partner doesn't feel criticized or attacked.

2. The Fact Finder. Describe the fridge situation like a neutral news reporter: "I see three leftover boxes and a single carrot." Goal: To state observable facts without using "loaded" words like "always" or "never" that trigger defensiveness.

3. The Positive Pitch. State your "Positive Need" like a luxury waiter: "I would love it if we could order those tacos we talked about last week." Goal: To clearly ask for what you do want rather than complaining about what you don't want.

4. The 24-Hour "Yes" Streak. Spend the next day making every minor request in this "Gentle" voice to see if your partner responds more positively. Goal: To turn the "Soft Start-up" into a natural habit that prevents small frictions from turning into big fights.

activities.name: The Investigator
activities.description: Understand your partner's experience and perspective.
activities.detailedDescription: The goal isn't to agree; it's to understand. This level uses "Reflective Listening" to ensure both of you feel seen and respected.
steps: 1. The "Lead Witness" Statement. One partner acts as the Witness, sharing a minor frustration for 2 minutes while the "Investigator" listens in total silence. Goal: To give one partner the floor to express their full perspective without the other person building a defense.

2. The "Evidence Recap." The Investigator must summarize the testimony: "So, the evidence suggests you feel stressed when the mail piles up... do I have my facts straight?" Goal: To ensure the "impact" of the message matches the "intent" and that the speaker feels accurately heard.

3. The "Case Closed" Validation. The Investigator looks for the logic in the story and says: "It makes sense that you'd feel [Emotion] given the situation." Goal: To provide emotional validation, which lowers tension even if you don't agree with the facts.
4. The Role Reversal. Swap the badge and the notebook so the other partner can be the Witness. Goal: To build a mutual "Empathy Bridge" where both partners feel their internal world is respected.

activities.name: The Great Compromise
activities.description: Shift from 'me vs you' to 'us vs the problem'.
activities.detailedDescription: This level moves you both from "Me vs. You" to "Us vs. The Problem" by finding common ground while protecting core needs.
steps: 1. The Treasure Chest (Non-Negotiables). Write down 3 "Core Needs" you cannot compromise on because they affect your values or self-respect. Goal: To identify the "must-haves" that are essential for you to feel safe and respected in the relationship.

2. The Suitcase (Flex-Zones). List 3 areas where you are willing to be flexible or try your partner's way for the sake of the team. Goal: To find the "wiggle room" where you can offer concessions without losing your sense of self.

3. The Island Charter. Create a "Third Way" solution for a conflict that keeps both people's "Treasure" safe while using "Suitcase" items to bridge the gap. Goal: To shift the dynamic into a creative partnership where you build a solution that works for both people.

4. The 7-Day Island Trial. Live by your new "Charter" for a week as an experiment rather than a permanent commitment. Goal: To lower the pressure of "fixing it forever" by testing a new behavior in a short, manageable sprint.

activities.name: The Circuit Breaker
activities.description: Identify and break negative communication cycles.
activities.detailedDescription: This level helps in realizing that the "Argument Pattern" is the real enemy and teaches you how to call a healthy time-out.
steps: 1. Name the "Glitch." Identify your typical fight pattern—like one person "chasing" and the other "running"—and give it a funny name like "The Volume War." Goal: To externalize the conflict so you realize "The Cycle" is the enemy, not your partner.

2. The Red Light Sensor. Learn to spot "Flooding" (sweaty palms or a fast heart) and call out a "Red Light!" the moment it happens. Goal: To recognize when your body is in "fight or flight" mode and your brain can no longer think logically.

3. The "Pause Menu" Ritual. Use a code word like "Marshmallow" to take a 20-minute break in separate rooms to calm down. Goal: To allow your nervous systems to return to a resting state so you don't say things you'll later regret.

4. The Power-Up Hug. Re-enter the room and hold a hug for 30 seconds before trying to talk again. Goal: To use physical touch to signal to both of your brains that the "threat" is gone and you are safe together.

activities.name: Love Map Update
activities.description: Update your knowledge of your partner's world.
activities.detailedDescription: Update your knowledge of your partner's world. This level turns you both into explorers rediscovering each other's current inner landscape.
steps: 1. The Curiosity Spark. Both of you take turns answering: "If you could start any hobby or interest right now without worrying about money or time, what would it be?" Goal: To replace old assumptions with fresh knowledge of who your partner is today.

2. The "Boring" Detail Exchange. Trade one tiny, random fact about your day that usually feels too "small" to mention. Ask each other: "Did anything weird or funny happen today that I haven't heard yet?" Goal: To rebuild the habit of sharing the little bits of life that create closeness.

3. The Deep Dive Duo. When one person shares a story, the other must ask: "What was the best part of that for you?" or "How did that make you feel?" then swap roles. Goal: To stop "autopilot" listening and show genuine interest in each other's thoughts.
4. The Big Reveal. Together, finish this sentence: "One thing I just learned about you that I never realized before is..." Goal: To acknowledge that your partner is still a mystery worth solving.

activities.name: The Dopamine Drop
activities.description: Do something new to trigger first-date energy.
activities.detailedDescription: Do something new together to trigger first-date energy. This level uses novelty to release the same "honeymoon" chemicals found in early dating.
steps: 1. The Adventure Blueprint. Grab a single piece of paper and together list 5 fun things you both want to experience as a couple before the year ends. Goal: To build "future-oriented excitement" and a shared vision to look forward to.
2. The "Beginner" Challenge. Find a quick game or a silly task (like a 30-second drawing contest) and try it together right now. Goal: To create a "shared struggle" that forces you to work as a team.
3. The 6-Second Connection. Put everything else aside and share a kiss that lasts at least six seconds. Goal: To use physical touch to lower stress and boost "bonding" hormones like oxytocin.
4. The Random Walk. Head to the door together and let a coin flip decide if you turn left or right at the first corner. Goal: To introduce "unplanned novelty" that breaks your daily routine.

activities.name: Bid-Catching Pro
activities.description: Notice and respond to small connection bids.
activities.detailedDescription: Notice and respond to small connection bids from each other. This level trains both of you to be each other's biggest fans.
steps: 1. The "Caught You" Shout-out. Both of you name one specific thing the other did recently that you really appreciated. Goal: To shift the relationship vibe toward more positive interactions.

2. The Origin Story Rewind. Take turns describing the exact moment or the first date when you both realized this relationship was something special. Goal: To revisit the original "spark" and build fondness.

3. The "Public" Brag. While standing together, tell a pet, a friend, or even send a quick text to a family member about one thing your partner is truly amazing at. Goal: To make each other feel valued and publicly respected.
4. The Gratitude Triple-Play. Exchange three specific things about "Us" as a couple that you are both feeling extra thankful for right now. Goal: To end the session feeling lucky to be together.

activities.name: The Intimacy Architect
activities.description: Build a daily ritual that bonds you deeply.
activities.detailedDescription: Build a daily ritual that bonds you deeply. This level moves you from being individuals to a unified "Us."
steps: 1. The "Vault" Secret Code. Brainstorm together to create a new, silly secret nickname or code word that belongs strictly to your team. Goal: To create a "private world" that only the two of you inhabit.

2. The Digital Blackout. Both of you agree to put your phones in another room right now and just exist in the same space without distractions. Goal: To ensure you are giving each other 100% of your attention.
3. The Team Motto Manifesto. Together, choose three words that describe who you are when you are at your absolute best as a couple. Goal: To solidify your identity as a partnership.
4. The Honeymoon Re-Up. Both of you share: "What is one thing we did in this session that made you feel closer to me?" Goal: To celebrate your progress and commit to staying "in the honeymoon."

activities.name: The Battery Check
activities.description: Check their energy before unloading your day.
activities.detailedDescription: Stop the "information dump" before it starts! This level turns checking in into a team strategy so you both feel heard without draining your "charge."
steps: 1. The Power Sync. Sit together and ask: "On a scale of 1% to 100%, how much social energy do you have left in your tank right now?" Goal: To realize that if your partner is at 5%, it's probably not the best time to talk about the budget.

2. The "Headlines" Only. Take turns sharing just three "news headlines" about your day (the high, the low, and the weird) without the full story yet. Goal: To give each other a "preview" of your internal state so you can decide together what needs a deep dive.

3. The Help Menu. Ask each other: "For the things I want to talk about, do you want me to just listen, or are we in 'Fix-It' mode?" Goal: To align your expectations so one person doesn't feel lectured when they just wanted a vent session.

4. The Unified Recharge. Pick one quick thing to do together right now to boost your "battery" scores (like a 30-second dance party or a snack). Goal: To manage each other's stress levels as a team before diving into a real talk.

activities.name: No-Gatekeeping Needs
activities.description: Say what you need—no hints, no guessing.
activities.detailedDescription: Retire the guessing games. This level turns "expressing needs" into a collaborative map-making session where you both tell it like it is.
steps: 1. The "I" Statement Swap. Use this fun fill-in-the-blank together: "I feel [Emotion] when [Event] happens, and it would be awesome if [Positive Need]." Goal: To practice bringing up topics without pointing fingers or making the other person feel attacked.

2. The Mind-Reader Resignation. Both of you admit one thing you wish your partner just "knew" without being told, then say it out loud clearly. Goal: To kill the "if they loved me, they'd know" myth and replace it with clear, kind directions.
3. The Secret Manual. Tell your partner: "I feel most loved/supported by you when you [Specific Action]." Goal: To give your partner a "cheat code" on how to be a hero in your world.

4. The "No-Hing" Pact. Agree on a funny code word you can both use when you feel like the other person is hinting instead of asking. Goal: To catch the habit of being indirect and bring the conversation back to total honesty.

activities.name: The Echo Chamber
activities.description: Repeat to prove you're really listening.
activities.detailedDescription: Prove you are on the same team. This level gamifies "Reflective Listening" so that neither person feels ignored or misunderstood.
steps: 1. The 60-Second Solo. One person talks about a minor frustration while the other listens without saying a single word or even thinking of a comeback. Goal: To practice "generous listening" where the only objective is to understand your partner's world.

2. The "Echo" Check. The listener must repeat back what they heard: "So, it sounds like you're feeling... because... did I get that right?" Goal: To ensure the message you sent is the one your partner actually received.

3. The Validation High-Five. The speaker can only move on once they feel 100% understood—if not, the listener tries the summary again! Goal: To build a bridge where both partners feel their feelings make sense, even if you don't agree with the logic.
4. The Role Swap. Switch who is talking and who is "echoing." Goal: To ensure communication is a two-way street, not a monologue.

activities.name: Safe Space Protocol
activities.description: Create a no-judgment zone for honesty.
activities.detailedDescription: Build your "No-Judgment Zone." This level creates the rituals that keep your communication safe and honest for the long haul.
steps: 1. The "Red Light/Green Light" Ritual. Together, identify "Triggers" (like being hangry or exhausted) that mean you should put a "Red Light" on serious talks. Goal: To protect the relationship by only having tough talks when you are both "Green Lights."

2. The Digital Blackout. Both of you put your phones in another room right now and just sit face-to-face for a few minutes. Goal: To show each other that the person in front of you is more important than whatever is on the screen.

3. The "Reset" Signal. Design a secret signal (like a specific hug or a funny phrase) to use when a talk gets too heated and you both need a "Pause." Goal: To recognize when "The Loop" of a fight is starting so you can stop it as a team.

4. The Communication Toast. Share one thing you both did in this session that made you feel more connected and celebrate with a high-five or a hug. Goal: To celebrate the work you've done and commit to keeping the "Safe Space" open every day.
